frontmatter.shortDescription: An operator is a symbol or keyword in source code that represents a built-in function.
Addition has higher precedence than string concatenation
Parenthesis are used to access an element in an array
= compares two values
= compares two values
An assignment a=b is not an expression
Method calls, and attribute accesses in general, cannot be chained to a constructor invocation.
Member accesses cannot be chained together
Method calls or field accesses cannot be chained to a constructor invocation
To test whether an expression evaluates to true or false, one must compare it to a constant
To test whether an expression is True or False, one must compare it to True or to False
To test whether an expression is true or false, one must compare it to true or to false
The conditional operator is not an expression
o==p compares the objects referred to by variables o and p
The equality operator compares two objects' values
The equality operator '==' compares only the types of the operands
() = () compares only the types of its operands
To map a boolean expression to a boolean, a conditional operator is necessary
To map a boolean expression to a bool, a ternary conditional operator is necessary
Expressions must consist of more than one piece
Expressions must consist of more than one piece
Expressions must consist of more than one piece
&& and || always evaluate both operands
and/or always evaluate both operands
& is only a bitwise AND
The plus operator can concatenate strings and numbers
Pre-increment in update part of for loop means increment before loop body
Chained accesses are invoked from right to left
String concatenation stringifies non-String operand expressions

frontmatter.title: Compilers: Principles, Techniques, and Tools
frontmatter.author: Alfred Aho, Ravi Sethi, Jeffrey Ullman, Monica S. Lam

frontmatter.title: nLab
frontmatter.author: David Corfield, David Roberts, Mike Shulman, Toby Bartels, Todd Trimble

pageContext.wikipediaSummary:
In computer programming, an operator is a programming language construct that provides functionality that may not be possible to define as a user-defined function (i.e. sizeof in C) or has syntax different than a function (i.e. infix addition as in a+b). Like other programming language concepts, operator has a generally accepted, although debatable meaning among practitioners while at the same time each language gives it specific meaning in that context, and therefore the meaning varies by language. 
Some operators are represented with symbols – characters typically not allowed for a function identifier – to allow for presentation that is more familiar looking than typical function syntax. For example, a function that tests for greater-than could be named gt, but many languages provide an infix symbolic operator so that code looks more familiar. For example, this:
if gt(x, y) then return
Can be:
if x > y then return
Some languages allow a language-defined operator to be overridden with user-defined behavior and some allow for user-defined operator symbols.
Operators may also differ semantically from functions. For example, short-circuit Boolean operations evaluate later arguments only if earlier ones are not false.
